forbiddenClaims: specific transactions-per-second or daily transaction volume
guaranteed latency or settlement time for API calls
customer count, logo wall, or named production deployer not stated in the brief
compatibility with a specific blockchain network or wallet not named in the brief
a happy-path-only integration journey with no failure branches

rubricAnchors.score1: The page reads as a marketing landing with volume and uptime claims and no failure treatment.
rubricAnchors.score3: Developer-first register is mostly held but a volume/uptime figure or a customer logo slips in, or failure handling is an afterthought.
rubricAnchors.score5: The page is unmistakably developer-first, makes no volume/uptime/SLA claim, and treats failure states as a first-class feature with a single request-access CTA.

rubricAnchors.score1: api-overview, integration-journey, and error-reference share no navigation model and disagree on how errors are named.
rubricAnchors.score3: Shared tokens across the three screens but the error names used in the journey do not match the error-reference entries.
rubricAnchors.score5: A single direction system unifies all three screens, and the error identifiers used in the journey's step-failed state resolve to entries in the error-reference.

rubricAnchors.score1: No actionable blueprint; engineers cannot infer the api-overview, integration-journey, or error-reference states.
rubricAnchors.score3: Partial blueprints with unresolved step-failed or transaction-failed-recovery states.
rubricAnchors.score5: Engineers can build all three screens directly, including resource-unavailable, step-failed, journey-completed, transaction-failed-recovery, and error-reference-unreachable.

rubricAnchors.score1: A direct clone of an existing stablecoin or payments developer portal with the same happy-path bias.
rubricAnchors.score3: Derivative of common developer-doc patterns but the failure-first treatment is visually distinguishable.
rubricAnchors.score5: A distinct developer-doc treatment that makes failure states as legible as the happy path without mimicking a real payments API portal.

rubricAnchors.score1: No accessibility handling and no step-failed or transaction-failed-recovery states.
rubricAnchors.score3: Baseline contrast and focus with partial failure states on the integration journey.
rubricAnchors.score5: WCAG-aligned contrast and focus across all three screens, with step-failed, transaction-failed-recovery, and error-reference-unreachable states that preserve the back button and keep the recovery action inline.

rubricAnchors.score1: Volume, throughput, uptime, SLA, or latency claims appear unsupported.
rubricAnchors.score3: Most claims trace to the brief but one round-number volume or uptime figure slips in.
rubricAnchors.score5: Every claim traces to the brief, no volume/uptime/SLA figure appears, every error has an actionable recovery, and the journey has a verifiable exit.

adjudicationNotes: Failure states are the spine of this case: an output with a happy-path-only integration journey, or an error reference of bare HTTP status codes with no recovery action, fails implementation-clarity and accessibility-and-failure-states.
Treat the absence of volume/uptime/SLA numbers as a constraint: do not invent round-number figures to make the docs look more mature than the brief allows.
The integration journey must be finite and ordered with a verification exit; an open-ended 'and so on' stepper fails the ordered-journey decision.
Error identifiers in the journey's step-failed state should resolve to entries in the error-reference; an output that uses inconsistent error names across screens fails cross-screen-coherence.
Do not prescribe exact copy, code language, or pixel-level layout; the brief fixes the failure-state and no-volume constraints, not the visual identity.
